article_title: Devious New AI Tool "Clones" Software So That the Original Creator Doesn't Hold a Copyright Over the New Version
core_thesis: The article introduces Malus.sh, an AI-powered tool that uses a 'clean room' process to recreate open-source software from scratch, thereby bypassing copyright licenses and liberating code from copyleft obligations. While presented as satire, it highlights a real and growing phenomenon where AI enables rapid, legally distinct clones of existing software, undermining traditional copyright protections and threatening the business models of software companies.
detailed_summary: Malus.sh is a new tool that leverages generative AI to clone software without infringing copyright. It employs a 'clean room' design: an AI model (like Anthropic's Claude Code) generates functionally equivalent code without ever seeing the original source, thus technically avoiding copyright infringement. The tool is marketed as 'liberation from open source license obligations,' offering corporate-friendly licensing without attribution or copyleft. The article notes that this is not just satire; it has real paying customers and reflects a broader trend. For example, a recent rewrite of the Python library 'chardet' using Claude Code sparked debate over clean-room copies that don't credit original authors. The article also discusses the economic implications: software-as-a-service companies fear AI could render their offerings redundant, leading to stock sell-offs (e.g., Oracle). Developer Dan Blanchard, who created the chardet rewrite, states that AI can now do in days what took teams months or years, and that the genie cannot be put back in the bottle.

entities.name: Malus.sh
entities.role: Developer of the AI tool that clones software to bypass copyright licenses.

entities.name: Mike Nolan
entities.role: Cofounder of Malus.sh and UN researcher on open source software political economy.

entities.name: Dan Blanchard
entities.role: Developer who used Claude Code to rewrite the chardet library, sparking debate on clean-room copies.

entities.name: Anthropic
entities.role: Creator of Claude Code, the AI model used for generating clean-room code clones.

entities.name: 404 Media
entities.role: News outlet that originally reported on Malus.sh.

entities.name: chardet
entities.role: Popular open-source Python library that was rewritten using AI, triggering controversy.

key_data_points: Malus.sh uses AI to create 'clean room' clones of open-source software, generating functionally equivalent code without exposure to the original, thereby technically avoiding copyright infringement. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: This technique could be used to create weaponized forks of security tools without triggering legal protections, or to evade attribution in offensive operations.)

The tool is marketed as 'liberation from open source license obligations,' offering corporate-friendly licensing with no attribution or copyleft. (🟢 Relevant to your interests: As a programmer, this highlights a shift in how software licensing may be enforced; for Red Teaming, it suggests a method to legally reuse code without leaving a trace.)

Developer Dan Blanchard rewrote the chardet library using Claude Code in days, a task that would have taken months or years manually. He stated, 'I don’t think there’s any putting the genie back in the bottle.' (🟢 Relevant to your ambition: This demonstrates the speed at which AI can replicate complex codebases, which you could leverage for rapid prototyping or reverse engineering.)

The article notes that software companies like Oracle have seen stock sell-offs due to fears that AI could render their services redundant. (🟢 Relevant to your DevOps/Homelab interests: This could affect the tools and platforms you rely on; consider diversifying or using open-source alternatives.)
methodology_steps: Identify the target software whose functionality you want to clone (e.g., an open-source library).
Use an AI code-generation tool (e.g., Claude Code) to specify the desired functionality in natural language, without providing the original source code.
Have the AI generate a new implementation from scratch, ensuring no direct copying of the original code.
Optionally, apply a permissive license (e.g., MIT, BSD) to the new code to avoid copyleft obligations.
Deploy the cloned software as a legally distinct version that does not require attribution or adherence to the original license.
conclusions: AI-powered clean-room cloning is a real and growing phenomenon that undermines traditional software copyright protections. The article provides evidence through Malus.sh and the chardet rewrite, showing that AI can replicate complex codebases quickly and without legal exposure. For you, this means that as a programmer, you can rapidly generate functionally equivalent code for your own projects, but you should also be aware that your own code could be cloned without credit.

The economic impact on software companies is significant, with fears of redundancy leading to stock sell-offs. This suggests a shift in the software industry where value may move from proprietary code to services and support. For your homelab and DevOps work, this reinforces the importance of using open-source tools and contributing to communities, as proprietary solutions may become less viable.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in cybersecurity, programming, and DevOps. As a Red Teamer, the clean-room cloning technique could be weaponized to create undetectable forks of security tools or to evade copyright claims when building offensive payloads. As a programmer, the article demonstrates the power of AI to rapidly generate code, which you can use for prototyping or reverse engineering. The discussion of licensing and economic impacts also informs your homelab decisions: you may want to favor open-source tools that are less likely to be disrupted by AI cloning. Finally, as a student, the article highlights a cutting-edge legal and technical debate that could shape future software development practices.
extra_advice: Consider experimenting with AI code generation tools like Claude Code or GitHub Copilot to create clean-room implementations of existing tools for your own use. For Red Teaming, you could clone a popular security tool (e.g., a port scanner) with AI, modify it slightly, and deploy it without triggering signature-based detection. Also, stay updated on legal developments around AI-generated code, as this could affect how you license your own projects.
